metadata.title: Why Gravity Makes Big Things Round
metadata.color_semantics: TEAL = hydrostatic equilibrium / round bodies / pressure exceeds strength; CRIMSON = small bodies / irregular / strength-dominated / rock wins

beats.narration_text: med davy A-I. From Sanskrit — meaning intelligent, or intellectually brilliant. The perfect name for our A-I-powered intelligent learning systems.

props.headline: Medhavy AI
props.body:
मेधावी (Medhavy): From Sanskrit, meaning
"intelligent" or "intellectually brilliant"
— the perfect name for our AI-powered
intelligent learning systems.

beats.narration_text: Phobos, moon of Mars, looks like a lumpy potato. Ceres, the largest asteroid, is nearly a perfect sphere. The same gravity that holds them together shapes one into a blob and the other into a ball. Why?

card.copy: Why Gravity Makes Big Things Round

beats.narration_text: For a large enough body, gravity at the center overwhelms the strength of rock. Material flows until the lowest-energy shape — a sphere — is reached. This is hydrostatic equilibrium.

production_viz.label: Hydrostatic equilibrium: gravity overwhelms rock strength → sphere

beats.narration_text: At what size does gravity win over material strength? And what determines the threshold between potato-shaped and spherical?

card.copy: Phobos: lumpy. Ceres: round. Same physics. What is the size threshold where gravity overcomes rock strength?

beats.narration_text: The central pressure of a uniform self-gravitating sphere: P equals two-pi over three times G times rho squared times R squared. Pressure grows as the square of radius. Rock has a compressive strength — a yield stress — that sets the maximum supported pressure.

For rocky material, yield stress is roughly 10 to the 7 to 10 to the 8 pascals. Equate central pressure to yield stress and solve for R. The answer: roughly 200 to 400 kilometers, depending on composition and density.

production_viz.label: Yield stress ~10^7–10^8 Pa → threshold R ≈ 200–400 km for rock

beats.narration_text: Phobos has a mean radius of about 11 kilometers — far below threshold. Rock strength supports the irregular shape indefinitely. Gravity simply is not strong enough to round it.

production_viz.label: Phobos: R = 11 km << 200 km threshold → rock wins, stays lumpy

beats.narration_text: Ceres has a mean radius of about 470 kilometers — above threshold. Ice and rock at those pressures cannot maintain large deviations from spherical. Over geologic time, Ceres flowed into its current near-round shape.

production_viz.label: Ceres: R = 470 km > threshold → gravity wins, rounds out over time

beats.narration_text: The IAU definition of dwarf planet requires hydrostatic equilibrium — roughly spherical shape. This is exactly the gravity-versus-strength threshold. Ceres qualifies; Phobos does not. The boundary is physical, not arbitrary.

production_viz.label: IAU dwarf planet = hydrostatic equilibrium = gravity-strength threshold

beats.narration_text: Icy bodies have a lower threshold than rocky ones because ice has a lower yield stress. Icy moons of around 100 to 200 kilometers radius can achieve roundness. Rocky bodies need to be larger.

production_viz.label: Ice: lower yield stress → threshold ~100–200 km; rock needs larger size

beats.narration_text: P center equals two-pi over three times G rho squared R squared. Threshold: about 200 to 400 km for rock. Phobos at 11 km: gravity loses, stays lumpy. Ceres at 470 km: gravity wins, rounds out. IAU dwarf-planet criterion maps exactly to this threshold.

card.copy: P_center ∝ R². Threshold ~200–400 km (rock). Phobos at 11 km: lumpy. Ceres at 470 km: round. IAU dwarf-planet = hydrostatic equilibrium = this threshold.

beats.narration_text: med davy. A-I-powered intelligent learning systems. On YouTube, at med davy A-I. med davy dot com.
